I assume this is retries for a redirect? If so, you might want to be a bit more explicit like:

```
retries = False
if redirect:
    retries = urllib3.util.Retry(total=False, redirect=1)
```

Otherwise, that will do `Retry.from_int` which, if I am remembering correctly, sets the total number of retries. I don't know if you care about connection errors and other things being retried instead of redirects.

Hmm, seems ok to me:

```Python
In [1]: import numpy as np

In [2]: import scipy.interpolate

In [3]: x = np.linspace(.2, 1.1, 10)

In [4]: y = np.random.random_integers(2, 5, 10)

In [5]: f = scipy.interpolate.interp1d(x, y, kind='slinear', fill_value=10., bounds_error=False)

In [6]: f(.15)
Out[6]: array(10.0)
```

Is that not what you'd expect?

> At any rate, the discontinuity here doesn't bother me too much.

Well, not explicitly zeroing out doesn't sound *worse*, right?  Unless there is some nasty behavior I am not seeing, it seems like interpolate will already be zeroing out things.

Given that we're now "owning this" and we want to document it explicitly, I think it's prudent that instead of copying it with `copy.copy`, that we make it explicitly defined here:

```
ALLOWED_PROTOCOLS = [u'ed2k', u'ftp', u'http', u'https', u'irc', u'mailto', u'news', u'gopher', u'nntp', u'telnet', u'webcal', u'xmpp', u'callto', u'feed', u'urn', u'aim', u'rsync', u'tag', u'ssh', u'sftp', u'rtsp', u'afs', u'data']
```

That way our list won't change between versions of html5lib and bleach can explicitly declare what we think is appropriate regardless of what html5lib says.

Related to this, I'm not sure I'd consider that list a list of "safe protocols". I think I'd want to trim it down to a much smaller subset. Maybe this much more conservative set:

```
ALLOWED_PROTOCOLS = [u'http', u'https', u'mailto']
```

Three overlapping critiques here:

1. Only use `_` for values that are discarded. In this case, the values are collected in the array, so you would use e.g. `np.array([scale for scale in range(min_scale, max_scale, 2)])`
2. But actually, you're just converting to a list, so instead, prefer `np.array(list(range(min_scale, max_scale, 2)))`
3. **But, actually,** you're just converting to an array, so instead use `np.arange(min_scale, max_scale, 2)`.

I totally understand the motivation. From all the existing params I've taken only `language` as the one available for most/all geocoders (I guess) and `additional_data` as this allows for getting shapes, too, which I think is rather unusual. The problem is: there are *many* more params, and only `additional_data` can have [17 different keys](https://developer.here.com/documentation/geocoder/topics/resource-params-additional.html). For the geocode API there are [28 more](https://developer.here.com/documentation/geocoder/topics/resource-geocode.html), plus [21 for the reverse geocode API](https://developer.here.com/documentation/geocoder/topics/resource-reverse-geocode.html)! And I haven't even added the multi-reverse geocode and landmark geocode APIs... (see FIXMEs).

So I felt like having an `other` parameter is the most efficient way of getting something out quickly, being well aware that this can not be documented nicely inside geopy, but only on the external REST API documentation. In this case I was told this is unlikely to change since there will be a new generation of these HERE APIs under a different label, anyway, but I'll double-check, again. So, if possible I'd really avoid putting in such a huge amount of work, but instead try to add a few more interesting test cases. Would that be an option? How is that handled for other rich APIs?

Hmm...  When I try timing this, regular min is ~35x faster (though both are really fast):

```
>>>timeit min(1.0, 2.0)
10000000 loops, best of 3: 149 ns per loop
>>>timeit np.min([1.0, 2.0])
100000 loops, best of 3: 5.24 µs per loop
```

Doing this after the calculation of the full size image removes a big part of the benefit of this parameter.  The point was mostly to save time during the calculation of the image -- to not make so large an fft when building the optical PSF if we don't actually need the full image.  I don't think the running time is much different if you shrink the image at this point for the input to the InterpolatedImage.

Is there some reason that you think that was invalid and switched to doing it this way?  If so, maybe we just ought to remove this parameter as an option at all, since I don't think it's very helpful this way.

I was thinking `diam` in meters, `lam` in nm (good point about `lambda`), and then let the code convert the ratio to arcsec for you.  Both numbers are easy for people to get right for a particular optical system, whereas lam_over_diam requires some significant documentation to explain how to calculate it.

The only semi-downside of this option is that is forces people into using arcsec for their world coordinate units if they are using OpticalPSF, but I think that's fine.  It's what we normally use anyway, and it's what Rachel implicitly did here for ChromaticOpticalPSF, which I also think was a perfectly reasonable thing to do.

(1) I ran BBDuk without any parameters to obtain the full set of supported parameters. Note that the version of BBDuk installed in our RNA-seq Docker image is newer than the version on GitHub that you linked which may be the reason why you couldn't find some parameters. Process inputs follow the same order as parameters displayed in the command line and are grouped similarly as well. I agree that matching command-line options with descriptions of inputs in the process is not always trivial. I can (a) add names of command-line parameters to descriptions or (b) change the names of process inputs to match the names of command-line parameters. However, (a) I didn't want the process input schema to look too technical and arouse a command-line-like feeling. Many of our users want to move away from the command line. (b) BBDuk uses a non-standard parameter naming conventions which doesn't play well with the rest of our processes. Lastly, imagine a refactoring where process inputs are recipes-like user-friendly and cannot be mapped one-to-one onto command-line parameters of the tool. I think that we should strive towards improving the descriptions of inputs to the point where the user would be able to use the process without the manual.

(2) This refactoring closely follows the function of the underlying tool. For example, if you call the process without parameters (except reads) it behaves similarly to the tool called from the command-line with just reads. This provides full control of operation and guarantees basic predictability of results: if an input in the process is not set explicitly, the tool's default value is used. This (a) eases integration into workflows because only the parameters explicitly set there have an effect on the operation of the tool and the process doesn't do anything unexpected automatically. This also (b) keeps power users in full control to achieve exactly what they want. The process is not intended to be used by basic users. For them we have recipes, workflows, and easy to use meta-processes named _for biologists_.

Custom input with adapters provided by BBDuk has been removed due to redundancy. The idea is to make those adapters available as public resources on our servers where they can be chosen as a regular data object by all trimming and filtering processes, including Trimmomatic and Cutadapt.

(3) If only reads are provided, they are copied to output and analyzed by FastQC. A warning is displayed that reads remain unmodified. This solution is based on what we've recently implemented in Trimmomatic and follows the operation of the tool itself which also prints out an equivalent warning.

Which parameters are important and which not is to some extent subjective so I decided to hide all non-essential parameters by default which gives a clean and non-intimidating interface to start with. To enable/show reference sequence-related parameters only after such sequences are provided is certainly an interesting idea. I base my decision not to implement this now on the fact that the process is much more likely to be used within a workflow than directly. Given the limited amount of time I deem other tasks more essential but I'm open to implementing this in the future.

If you look at `java_thrift_library`, it takes an `exports` argument that allows users to say "I depend on 10 things, but only export 4 of them", similar to what you would do for non-generated code.

This is only really relevant when you have `strict_deps` enabled for your targets, but if you don't care either way, I'd recommend going conservative and not exporting much: a dependent can always just add an explicit dependency, and it's much easier to loosen the constraints later than it is to tighten them.

I think it ought to work like this: A has C on its compile classpath. Given an X depending on A, X won't have C on its compile classpath because A doesn't export B or C. If A exports B, then C will be on X's classpath.

```
A -d-> B
B -e-> C
X -d-> A

cp(A) == {B, C}
cp(X) == {A}

# if we add
A -e-> B
# then
cp(X) == {A, B, C}
```

> Here aLen = anOutputBufLen + anInputBufLen. Right?

Absolutely not. The length of the transaction is determined solely by the SPI master. 

Also keep in mind that the contents of `anOutputBuf` go out at the same time that `anInputBuf` is bring written into. You would *never* add the lengths. With a SPI transaction of five bytes, you have potentially 5 bytes of output and 5 bytes of input.

I was thinking about the following scenario:

Broker 1 has partitions with size [ 200, 200, 10 ]
Broker 2 has partitions with size [ 300 ]

Both have disk capacity of 1000.

The ideal distribution would be [ 200, 200] and [300, 10 ]. But if we decide to move differently we could have [ 200, 10] and [300, 200] which made it worse.

What I mean is that in order to find the optimal packing of partitions, one should have access to all partitions sizes and do a packing algorithm. Only knowing about the imbalance would not be sufficient for writing an optimal algorithm.

Does it make sense? Writing lots of data might be costly and running such algorithm might be costly as well. Maybe we just want an approximated solution...
